items.rationale: He leads development of Jolt and believes its sumcheck/GKR/lookup approach is becoming the dominant paradigm; sees zkVMs as core scaling and verifiable-computation infrastructure for crypto.

citations.quote: Jolt is getting faster and the underlying techniques we've been advocating for — sumcheck, GKR, lookups — are becoming more popular. It's not a coincidence.
the best teams in crypto are betting their futures on Jolt behind the scenes.

items.rationale: Views a privacy-preserving unique-human ID as the necessary 'root of trust' to defend networks against AI bots, deepfakes, and impersonation, and to let humans and AI coexist online.
items.age_note: Held consistently since Jan 2024; repeatedly reaffirmed through June 2025.

citations.quote: I believe that World ID is the most promising as the root of trust.
Every social network will struggle. Every person will want a network where you can tell the difference.

items.rationale: Considers properly designed network tokens (ownership/value capture in a decentralized marketplace) the highest-value token category and the heart of the industry, distinct from company-backed tokens and memecoins.

citations.quote: Network tokens (the king 👑) vs. company-backed tokens (not so much).
We finally have the chance to build economically sustainable decentralized networks.

items.rationale: Sees clear and growing product-market fit for stablecoins (growth seemingly independent of interest rates) and believes the long-promised crypto payments stack is finally viable; wants more stablecoin experimentation.
items.age_note: Payments thesis dates to Apr 2024 but reinforced by recent (Apr 2025) stablecoin observations.

citations.quote: With increasingly obvious product-market fit for stablecoins, I expect more experiments here (no LUNA please).
stablecoin growth (including total supply, not pictured here) also seems relatively independent of interest rates. Very interesting!

items.rationale: Distinguishes diffusion from control; values systems that are 'locked open' with no single trusted controller (credible neutrality) as crypto's core benefit over corporate networks.

citations.quote: 'Decentralization' in this sense is a means to an end: a system that lacks a single controller that you need to trust.
Censorship resistance just means it's locked open.

items.rationale: Wants radical privacy as the default; argues institutions should use every cryptographic tool (ZK, private biometrics) to minimize honeypots of sensitive personal data.

citations.quote: We have the technology to make radical privacy the default. Let's build it.
Why are we forcing institutions to collect honeypots of sensitive, slowly-changing personal data?

items.rationale: Believes autonomous AI agents will transact, pay for their own compute, and need neutral portable on-chain identity — a major unsolved opportunity at the AI-crypto intersection.

citations.quote: Identity for agents is one of the most "obvious" future challenges for AI, yet there is still no clear effort to solve the problem.
AI agents can't have bank accounts, but they can have crypto wallets.

items.rationale: Calls the arcade-token model the most underappreciated token type — lets apps/games monetize and create user value with far fewer speculative risks than copying network tokens.

citations.quote: I called this the most underappreciated token type in crypto on the podcast recently.
Many interesting ways to monetize a game and create value for users with far fewer speculative risks.

items.rationale: Endorses Snapchain's data architecture as the right way for decentralized social networks to maximize programmability and performance while minimizing recentralization risk.

citations.quote: Snapchain is how I think decentralized social networks ought to store data to max programmability + performance and min the risks of recentralization.

items.rationale: Champions trust-minimized Ethereum verification in-browser and in wallets; ongoing personal investment in Helios improvements and getting light clients adopted by default.

citations.quote: Big update for your favorite Ethereum light client, Helios!
You can run a Helios light client in the browser to verify Ethereum L1 + OP Mainnet + Base. It works right now.

items.rationale: Believes crypto can now ship decentralized projects with network tokens without the two-company/foundation structure, cutting complexity and accelerating development.

citations.quote: It's time for crypto move on from the "foundation" model.
now we can cut complexity and accelerate dev without compromising decentralization.

items.rationale: Wants protocols that measure impact, attribute credit, and route payment to open-source authors — completing 'Git's evolution' into capitalism's feedback loop.

citations.quote: Merit Systems was founded to design this: a protocol for measuring impact, attributing credit, and routing payment to the authors creating value

items.rationale: Sees them as net-negative externalities: not technically interesting, they make crypto look like a casino, deter great founders, and give politicized regulators ammunition, setting the industry back.
items.age_note: Most forceful critiques are Apr 2024; the underlying stance is consistently reaffirmed via his 2025 token-classification work, not reversed.

citations.quote: we shouldn't pretend that the casino doesn't set us back. Deterring great founders and giving ammo to politicized regulators has a profound effect.
Memecoins alter how the public, regulators, and entrepreneurs see crypto. At best, it looks like a risky casino.

items.rationale: Expects all content to be assumed AI-generated unless cryptographically attested; sees behavioral bot-detection as already 'cooked,' threatening every social network without a sybil-resistant ID layer.
items.age_note: Originated Jan 2024; restated and intensified through May–June 2025.

citations.quote: advances in AI mean identifying humans through behavior alone is cooked.
The "mystery meat" scenario seems unavoidable

items.rationale: Warns farming is fully professionalized and AI now fabricates lifelike on-chain histories, so airdrops risk leaking value to sybils unless defended by supply-verification or unique-human ID.

citations.quote: Airdrop and reward farming is completely professionalized. We don't appreciate its scale because every algorithmic feed struggles to hide it from us.

items.rationale: Concerned that tokens controlled by a single company (or with 'pie chart' tokenomics and fake governance) are mislabeled as network tokens — no real decentralization, value, or holder protection.

citations.quote: If the "tokenomics" is just a pie chart, there is no network, no potential to create value, and no point.
If there is only one company with actual control of the system... this is not a (decentralized) network token.

items.rationale: Laments PCs moving from direct downloads to app stores and corporate gatekeeping as a regression eroding the open web; control over the network is the source of gatekeeping power.

citations.quote: PC apps moving from direct downloads to app stores is a regression, cursed, tragic.
You don't own your username — or anything else — on a corporate network.

items.rationale: Argues closed-source AI drastically limits innovation and isn't demonstrably safer; prefers open (even uncensored) models, with safety provided by crypto tools rather than secrecy.
items.age_note: Articulated mainly mid-2024 (and Jan 2025 re: DeepSeek-R1 reasoning); not contradicted since but less recently restated.

citations.quote: Closed source drastically limits innovation from startups, universities, independent researchers, etc. And it makes it more difficult to keep software safe.

items.rationale: Treats ETH as ownership in a decentralized network-marketplace selling blockspace; value accrues via the EIP-1559 burn (effectively automatic buybacks), with demand independent of using ETH itself for gas.

citations.quote: The value accrues to ETH through the burn.
I think ETH is already doing (fully automatic) buybacks via the EIP-1559 burn.

items.rationale: Cites UNI as a 'clear' network token that can capture protocol fees (via mechanisms like UniStaker) from the Uniswap network-marketplace — his model example of legitimate token value capture.

citations.quote: UNI is a clear network token and we discuss the example very clearly.
Network tokens like ETH, SOL, and UNI can capture value from their underlying networks through protocol fees.

items.rationale: Grouped with ETH and UNI as a legitimate network token whose holders own/use a decentralized network no single company controls.

citations.quote: Many companies build on and use Ethereum, Solana, and Uniswap — but they're using the network, and none of them control it.

items.rationale: Respects Bitcoin as the native asset of arguably the most secure and decentralized network, giving it economic characteristics unlike any memecoin; pushes back on 'bitcoin is a memecoin' takes.

citations.quote: being the native asset of what is arguably the most secure and decentralized network gives it economic characteristics... So I don't think it's a memecoin.
It's incredible that we have bitcoin.

items.rationale: Strongly backs the World network as the leading private, sybil-resistant identity root of trust; views its token as bootstrapping the ID network and potentially capturing fees in future.
items.age_note: Token-specific framing is lighter; enthusiasm is for the World ID network, with token role described in May 2025.

citations.quote: The tokens are to help bootstrap the ID network, and in the future may capture fees automatically.
I believe that World ID is the most promising as the root of trust.

items.rationale: Names FTT as a canonical company-backed token whose value came uniquely from the company rather than a decentralized network — the failure mode he warns against.

citations.quote: FTT was one, there are others.

items.rationale: Uses LUNA as a cautionary tale when calling for renewed algorithmic-stablecoin experimentation; explicitly does not want a repeat.

citations.quote: I expect more experiments here (no LUNA please).

items.rationale: Classifies Dogecoin as a store-of-value/memecoin with no enterprise value (unlike network tokens with protocol revenue) — outside the value-creating designs he favors.

citations.quote: memecoins/store of value tokens like Dogecoin have no enterprise value (like gold).

risk_regime.rationale: Eddy is a structurally bullish builder: he repeatedly says there has never been a better time to build in crypto, citing better tech, clearer need, and a transformed regulatory environment. His risk-on posture is firmly on fundamentals and decentralized-network construction (ZK, identity, network tokens, stablecoins, payments), paired with strong risk-off on speculation (memecoins, company-backed tokens, casino dynamics). It is a long-horizon, fundamentals-first optimism rather than a tactical/market-timing bullishness.

notes: View is overwhelmingly that of a researcher/builder (a16z crypto CTO), not a trader — convictions reflect intensity of belief in technologies and token designs, not price calls. Strongest and most recent throughlines (2025): zkVMs/Jolt, private sybil-resistant identity as the 'root of trust' against AI bots/deepfakes, and a rigorous token taxonomy (network tokens = 'king'; arcade tokens underrated; company-backed tokens and memecoins disfavored). No major reversals detected in the corpus; the anti-memecoin stance and proof-of-personhood thesis are consistent from early 2024 through June 2025 and only intensify. The closed-source-AI concern is the least-recently restated (mid-2024) and is marked persisted. Where he discusses ETH valuation, he explicitly rejects simple commodity/DCF/'oil' analogies, framing L1 tokens as equity-like ownership in network-marketplaces.
